$comment: wezterm V1.2 wild scan — fifth wild V1.2 scan; first browser-extension entry in catalog.

repo_metadata.name: wezterm
repo_metadata.description: A GPU-accelerated cross-platform terminal emulator and multiplexer written by @wez and implemented in Rust

accounts.bio: WezTerm is a GPU-accelerated cross-platform terminal emulator and multiplexer written by @wez and implemented in Rust
Happy dev hacking configs and playing with Nix (:

dependabot_config.raw_snippet:
# Please see the documentation for all configuration options:
# https://help.github.com/github/administering-a-repository/configuration-options-for-dependency-updates

version: 2
updates:
  - package-ecosystem: "github-actions"
    directory: "/"
    schedule:
      interval: "monthly"

prs.title: Prevent "screen scraping", disable DECRQCRA
Fix COPR virtual package installation on Fedora 43
Fix parsing of partial SGR mouse sequences
Fix COPR build: expand TAR_NAME variable in BUILD_REQUIRES
Fix typo in `docs/scrollback.md`
Fix macOS notifications to display as toast popups
log: add a missing newline
ringlog: make printing to stderr faster
build(deps): bump lru from 0.12.5 to 0.16.3
Fix install script copy buttons not stripping `$` prefix
wezterm-blob-leases: Use uuid v4 over v1
Split RPM package into separate subpackages for wezterm-common, wezterm-gui and wezterm-mux-server
Fix: config max_fps
docs: add installation instructions for Fedora Atomic desktops
fix: memory leak in macOS MetalLayer management
Fix fractional scaling issues in hyprland 0.51.0
fix: missing `equals` cases in check condition.
termwiz: make ScreenSize and Blocking serde serializable
Update flake.lock
Revert "Fix crash on hidpi with wayland (#6508)"
Fix `mismatched_lifetime_syntaxes` warnings
Fix: Mild documentation corrections
Remove $RUST_BACKTRACE from Nix devShell
Prevent infinite loop in macOS' "Service" menu validation
Remove deprecated `NSWindowFullScreenButton`
Update flake.lock
improve pct_to_glyph algorithm for progress reporting
term: indicate support for OSC 52 in Primary DA response
Fix disable ligatures snippet
Don't update the nix flake dependencies _that_ often
Update flake.lock
nix: Update harfbuzz
Fix Nix build by disabling broken cargo-auditable step
Update flake.lock
fix: aliasing when using Center in window_content_alignment
Advertize the Su capability too
feat: Added support for window blur on KDE Wayland
docs: fix typos in SpawnCommand docs
docs: fix title of get_progress() page
feat(mark-last-tab): append last tab name with '*'
ci: cache api was biffed
symlink judiciously to make LaunchServices happy
Add progress bar glyphs
Add new graph branch glyphs
docs: Fix broken link in to_string.md
Parse all permutations of optional `P s` parameters for `CSI P s ; P s`
Make some termwiz dependency optional
fix: honor environment variables and cwd unconditionally for SpawnCommand
build(deps): bump cachix/cachix-action from 15 to 16
docs: Mention Nix install requires Git to be available

solo_maintainer.verbatim_sentence: Note: wez accounts for 97% of all commits. This is a solo-maintainer project — all governance findings should be read in that context.

entries.title: Solo-maintainer concentration — wez holds 97.2% of top-contributor commit share on a 25k-star privileged tool
entries.action_hint: Pin to a reviewed release tag; audit the Lua config you run since it can spawn subprocesses.
entries.threat_model_context: wez is the dominant author by a wide margin (7,346 commits of ~7,558 top-contributor total — 97.2%). There is no branch protection, no ruleset, no CODEOWNERS. wezterm handles SSH keys (wezterm-ssh), runs operator-authored Lua config that can spawn arbitrary subprocesses (run_child_process / background_child_process), and includes a multiplexer server that talks over Unix sockets. A credential compromise of wez's GitHub account reaches every user's terminal emulator, their SSH sessions, and any config-driven subprocess flow on the next release. The only reason this is Warning rather than Critical via C20 is the 26-month release stall (F1) — stale releases delay user-facing impact.
entries.what_this_means: The supply-chain blast radius for wezterm is substantial. Governance is effectively 'trust wez'. There is no structural second-human gate at any point in the commit-to-release path.
entries.what_this_does_not_mean: Not evidence of malicious behavior by wez. 8 years and 25k stars reflect genuine investment. The finding is structural — the review gate that would catch a compromised-account scenario is absent.
entries.how_to_fix: Maintainer-side: enable branch protection requiring ≥1 approving review; recruit at least one co-maintainer (bew, tzx, chipsenkbeil, jsgf, joexue — tail contributors — are plausible candidates) with CODEOWNERS entries; add a SECURITY.md documenting the disclosure channel. Consumer-side: pin to a specific reviewed release tag; audit any third-party Lua config plugins you load.

entries.title: 807-day release stall — latest tag 2024-02-03 despite main active through 2026-04-01
entries.action_hint: Users on the last stable release are running 2-year-old code. If this matters to you, track main via distro-nightly packages or build from source.
entries.threat_model_context: Last formal release was `20240203-110809-5046fc22` on 2024-02-03 (807 days before scan). Main is active — last push 2026-04-01, 19 days before scan. 237 open PRs, 1,452 open issues. The prior release cadence (2-month intervals) stopped abruptly after the Feb 2024 release. The consumer effect: whatever Windows Defender / Homebrew / winget / distro-stable serves IS that 2-year-old release, which is already superseded by substantial main-branch development. Fixes merged to main never reach users of the shipped version.
entries.what_this_means: If you installed wezterm via winget, homebrew (stable), or a distro stable package, you are running February 2024 code. Any security-relevant fix merged to main since then is not reaching your install.
entries.what_this_does_not_mean: Not evidence of abandonment — main is active. The gap is between active development and formal release, not an inactive repo.
entries.how_to_fix: Maintainer-side: cut a new release (even a date-tagged snapshot of main) so installed users receive accumulated bug fixes. Consumer-side: if you need recent main changes, use `cargo install --git https://github.com/wezterm/wezterm` or distro-nightly packages; otherwise accept the 2-year-old shipped version.

entries.title: No SECURITY.md + 0 published GHSA advisories in 8 years on a privileged tool
entries.action_hint: Disclosure path for a wezterm vulnerability you find is unclear — use GitHub's 'Report a vulnerability' button, do not post publicly.
entries.threat_model_context: community/profile reports has_security_policy=false. security-advisories count is 0. Over 8 years and 25k stars, either (a) no security-relevant issues have been discovered — implausible for a codebase this large handling SSH + Lua subprocess + multi-platform GUI; (b) fixes have shipped silently in releases; (c) issues have been addressed without any public disclosure. Reading (b) is consistent with the silent-fix pattern flagged on Xray-core (entry 19) and kronos (entry 17). The 807-day release stall (F1) adds an extra wrinkle — even if fixes ship to main, they don't reach users.
entries.what_this_means: No documented private channel for a vulnerability reporter to use. GitHub's built-in 'Report a vulnerability' UI may still work (not verified), but it is implicit rather than documented.
entries.what_this_does_not_mean: Not evidence of vulns being suppressed. Consistent with the single-maintainer pattern where disclosure formality lags behind capacity.
entries.how_to_fix: Maintainer-side: add a 1-page SECURITY.md naming the private-advisory channel and expected response window. Consumer-side: if you find something, use the private-advisory UI; do not post publicly.

entries.title: Dependabot tracks github-actions only — 70+ Cargo workspace crates have no automated CVE watch
entries.action_hint: Run `cargo audit` locally against Cargo.lock after building.
entries.threat_model_context: dependabot.yml is present and configured, but the `ecosystems_tracked` list contains only `github-actions`. The 70+ Cargo workspace sub-crates (E9) depend on many external crates with transitive dep graphs (serde, tokio, openssl, harfbuzz, cairo-sys-rs, fontconfig, freetype, etc.). A CVE published against any of these would not surface to the maintainer via dependabot. This is the same operational-gap pattern seen on kamal (entry 18, Ruby deps unwatched).
entries.what_this_means: Dep-graph CVEs in the Rust workspace require manual discovery (cargo audit, RustSec advisory-db monitoring).
entries.what_this_does_not_mean: Not evidence that a specific CVE is currently reachable in wezterm's dep graph. It's an operational gap, not a known vuln.
entries.how_to_fix: Maintainer-side: add `- package-ecosystem: cargo` to dependabot.yml. Consumer-side: `cargo audit` against a checked-out copy of the repo before trusting a self-build.

entries.title: 30-workflow CI pipeline with Windows/macOS/Linux builds — positive operational investment
entries.threat_model_context: 30 GitHub Actions workflows including Windows continuous, macOS continuous, tagged-release pipelines for both, nightly builds, pages deployment. The CI infrastructure is substantial — the release pipeline ends well short of emitting a release tag, but everything upstream of that is wired up. This is Info rather than Warning because it describes existing positive investment.
entries.what_this_means: The CI layer is healthy. The gap is specifically in the release-tag step (F1) and the governance-gate-before-merge step (F0).
entries.what_this_does_not_mean: Not a mitigation for F0 or F1 — CI doesn't substitute for human review or release cadence.
entries.how_to_fix: No fix required; the CI investment is a positive signal.

entries.title: Three scanner gaps specific to Rust workspace repos — Cargo parsing missing, workspace sub-crates mis-channelled, serde false positives
entries.action_hint: Cargo-specific audits (cargo audit, cargo-geiger) needed to fill these gaps locally.
entries.threat_model_context: Three harness gaps surfaced on this scan: (1) Cargo.lock parsing not implemented — runtime_count=0 and osv total_vulns=0 are coverage artifacts, not Rust-dep-clean reality (E10); (2) distribution-channel detection misinterpreted 70+ workspace sub-crates as 70 separate crates.io channels when the actual distribution is GitHub Releases binaries + homebrew + winget + distro packaging (E9); (3) dangerous_primitives deserialization regex produced 9 false positives on Rust serde `fn deserialize` trait implementations (E11). Same pattern as Go (Xray-core entry 19) and Ruby (kamal entry 18) — language-ecosystem-specific harness gaps.
entries.what_this_means: For this scan, Rust-specific risk assessments (dep CVEs, unsafe-code regions, release-asset inventory) are incomplete at the harness layer.
entries.what_this_does_not_mean: Not evidence about wezterm. A property of the scanner.
entries.how_to_fix: Scanner-side: (a) add Cargo.lock / Cargo.toml parsing with RustSec advisory-db lookup; (b) distinguish workspace sub-crates from publishable channels (reject sub-crates whose Cargo.toml lives below the workspace root); (c) tighten the deserialization regex to not match serde trait-method definitions. Consumer-side: run cargo audit locally.

entries.claim: wez is the dominant author — 7,346 commits out of ~7,558 top-contributor total (97.2% share). Next contributor (bew) has 65. Solo-maintainer concentration is extreme.
entries.result: wez: 7346; bew: 65; tzx: 55; chipsenkbeil: 34; jsgf: 30; joexue: 27. total_contributor_count: 100. Top-1 share: 97.2% — well above the 80% solo-maintainer threshold.

entries.claim: No branch protection of any kind — classic HTTP 404, 0 rulesets, 0 rules on default branch, no CODEOWNERS.
entries.result: classic: HTTP 404. rulesets: {entries: [], count: 0}. rules_on_default: {entries: [], count: 0}. CODEOWNERS: not found at any of CODEOWNERS, .github/CODEOWNERS, docs/CODEOWNERS, .gitlab/CODEOWNERS.

entries.claim: Last formal release was `20240203-110809-5046fc22` on 2024-02-03 — 807 days before scan. Prior releases followed a ~2-month cadence; then the release pipeline stopped even though main kept moving.
entries.result: total_count: 64. Most recent 3: 20240203-110809-5046fc22 (2024-02-03), 20240128-202157-1e552d76 (2024-01-29), 20240127-113634-bbcac864 (2024-01-27). Gap from Feb 2024 to scan date (2026-04-20) = 807 days. repo pushed_at: 2026-04-01 (19 days before scan) — main is active; the releases are stalled.

entries.claim: PR review posture on the 300 most recent closed PRs: formal_review_rate=3.3%, any_review_rate=32.7%, self_merge_count=6. any-review rate is meaningfully higher than many single-maintainer tools because wezterm has a Discord/forum community that comments on PRs.
entries.result: sample_size: 300; formal_review_rate: 3.3; any_review_rate: 32.7; self_merge_count: 6 (2.0%); total_merged_lifetime: large (lifetime PR count not reported by harness). Pattern: most PRs merged by wez after community discussion in issue/PR comments, without formal 'APPROVED' review decisions.

entries.claim: community/profile reports no SECURITY.md and no code_of_conduct; has_contributing=true. health_percentage: 62. security-advisories count: 0.
entries.result: health_percentage: 62; has_security_policy: false; has_contributing: true; has_code_of_conduct: false; license_spdx: NOASSERTION. security-advisories count: 0. No SECURITY.md despite 8 years of active development + 25k stars + privileged tool (SSH client + config-file Lua scripting).

entries.claim: 30 GitHub Actions workflows — the CI investment is substantial: Linux/macOS/Windows continuous builds, nightly builds, macOS and Windows tagged-release pipelines, pages deployment. No pull_request_target usage.
entries.result: workflows.count: 30. Examples: posix.yml (Linux builds), gen_macos_continuous.yml, gen_windows_continuous.yml, gen_macos_tag.yml, gen_windows_tag.yml, pages.yml. pull_request_target_count: 0. This is the opposite extreme from a tiny repo — the CI pipeline is heavily invested in, but the release step at the end of the pipeline has stalled.

entries.claim: Dependabot config is present but tracks only `github-actions`. Cargo (crates.io) ecosystem is NOT watched. The Rust workspace has 70+ sub-crates with transitive dep graphs that receive no automated CVE alerts via dependabot.
entries.result: dependabot.yml ecosystems_tracked: ['github-actions']. Bundler/crates.io absent. Schedule: present but CI-only. 70+ workspace crates rely on transitive deps (harfbuzz, cairo-sys-rs, fontconfig, freetype, openssl, serde, tokio, etc.) — each potentially carries CVEs that won't surface via dependabot.

entries.claim: Open security issue #5074 ('Winget installation flagged as a trojan by windows security') has been open 785 days. Body identifies the issue as Windows Defender FALSE-positively flagging the wezterm installer — it is a distribution-tooling issue, not an exploitable vulnerability.
entries.result: issue #5074 created 2024-02-25, state=open. Title: 'Winget installation flagged as a trojan by windows security'. Body reports Windows Defender flagging the 20240203 release installer as a trojan — almost certainly a Defender heuristic false-positive on an unsigned Windows binary. Not an RCE / vuln. Still, 785 days is an operational signal on its own. The V1.2.x-widened q2_oldest_open_security_item_age_days signal correctly fired (title matches 'security') but the underlying item is noise. Phase 4 Q2 color stays red not because of this issue but because of the 807-day release-stall (E3), which IS a real fix-to-user-latency concern.

entries.claim: wezterm is a Cargo workspace of 70+ sub-crates (wezterm-gui, wezterm-ssh, wezterm-term, termwiz, wezterm-config, etc.). Harness distribution-channel detection misinterpreted each workspace sub-crate as an independent crates.io-publishable package.
entries.result: Cargo workspace with 70+ members. Harness emitted 70+ crates-* entries in distribution_channels — none of which are the actual distribution channel for wezterm end-users (which is GitHub Releases binaries + distro packages + homebrew + winget). Only a handful of the sub-crates (termwiz notably) are actually published to crates.io.

entries.claim: Harness osv.dev query returned 0 vulns because the Rust crates ecosystem parser is not implemented (same gap pattern as Go/Ruby in earlier scans). runtime_count: 0 despite 70+ Cargo.toml files.
entries.result: osv total_vulns: 0. runtime_count: 0. dev_count: 0. Cargo.lock present in repo (detected as manifest) but not parsed. The actual dep-graph CVE surface is unknown; to determine it, run `cargo audit` locally against the installed Cargo.lock.

entries.claim: Dangerous-primitives deserialization family reported 9 hits — all are Rust serde `deserialize` trait implementations (e.g., `fn deserialize [de, D]` returning `Result` types with generic parameters), not unsafe deserialization of untrusted input. False-positive pattern on Rust serde idioms.
entries.result: 9 hits: wezterm-cell/src/image.rs (serde), wezterm-cell/src/lib.rs (serde), term/src/color.rs (serde Palette256), codec/src/lib.rs (comment mentioning serde), wezterm-blob-leases/src/lease.rs (serde), plus 4 more. All are serde `fn deserialize` type-class implementations — Rust trait methods with generic parameters. Not unsafe-deserialization findings.

entries.claim: wezterm's feature set includes an SSH client (`wezterm ssh user@host`), Lua-scriptable config with `wezterm.run_child_process` / `background_child_process` subprocess primitives, and multiplexer-server functionality (mux server on Unix sockets). Combined blast radius: SSH keys handled by Rust crate wezterm-ssh; operator config can arbitrarily spawn processes; multi-host session multiplexing.
entries.result: Lua API exposes: wezterm.run_child_process, wezterm.background_child_process, ExecDomain, SshDomain, WslDomain. The mux-server talks over a Unix socket. wezterm-ssh crate handles SSH authentication, including key-based auth. Feature set is large and legitimate; threat model is 'your terminal emulator has your SSH keys + your config-driven subprocesses'.

section_01.summary: wezterm is a 25k-star GPU-accelerated cross-platform terminal emulator + multiplexer + SSH client in Rust, maintained near-solo by @wez across 8 years. 30 GitHub Actions workflows build multi-platform binaries on every push — the CI investment is substantial. The gap is two-sided: (F0) no branch protection + 97.2% solo-maintainer share on a privileged tool, and (F1) an 807-day release stall despite main being active. Users installing from homebrew/winget/distro are running February-2024 code; contributors merging to main aren't reaching them. Plus a silent-disclosure pattern (F2, 0 advisories in 8 years). Caution rather than Critical because c20 downgrades given the stale release (the governance floor is blunted by the code not actually reaching users quickly).

section_02.pill_row: F0 Warning · F1 Warning · F2 Warning · F3 Info · F4 Info · F5 Info

section_06.summary: **Zero scorecard overrides** on this scan — all 4 Phase 4 colors match the Phase 3 advisory. First V1.2 wild scan with 0 overrides; useful V13-1 calibration data point (signal vocabulary is calibrating well for this shape post-V1.2.x widening). Q1 red via governance floor (genuine: solo-maintainer + no protection + 3.3% formal). Q2 red via V1.2.x-widened q2_oldest_open_security_item_age_days signal (issue #5074, 785 days — but note: #5074 is a Windows-Defender FP per E8; the real Q2 concern is the 807-day release stall, not the signal trigger). Q3 amber (SECURITY.md absent + 0 advisories, but has CONTRIBUTING). Q4 amber (no formal channel pinning; stale release).

split_axis_decision.reasoning: Single consumer profile — all wezterm users install the binary and use it as their terminal. The optional SSH-client and Lua-config features are available to all users uniformly; they are capability surface, not deployment-context.

groups.headline: Warning — F0 + F1 + F2 describe the governance + cadence posture
groups.one_line_summary: Solo-maintainer + 807-day release stall + no SECURITY.md on a 25k-star privileged tool.

items.claim: wez holds 97.2% top-contributor share; no branch protection, no CODEOWNERS; privileged tool (SSH + Lua subprocess).
Last formal release 2024-02-03 (807 days before scan). Main active through 2026-04-01. Users on stable are 2 years behind.
No SECURITY.md + 0 published advisories in 8 years despite privileged-tool threat model.

groups.headline: Info — F3 + F4 + F5 are context
groups.one_line_summary: Operational gaps and positive investment, plus scanner coverage.

items.claim: Dependabot watches github-actions only; 70+ Cargo workspace crates have no automated CVE watch.
30-workflow CI pipeline (Windows/macOS/Linux + nightly + tag) — substantial operational investment despite release gap.
Rust-ecosystem scanner gaps: Cargo parsing missing; workspace sub-crates mis-channelled; serde false positives.

selections.reason: If wez adds a CODEOWNERS + ruleset requiring ≥1 reviewer, the Q1 governance floor collapses and F0 severity drops without needing Phase 4 override.
A new release (even a date-tagged snapshot of main) closes F1, moves Q2 out of red, and reduces the 2-year consumer-exposure window. Single highest-value remediation.
Adding `cargo` to dependabot ecosystems gives the maintainer automated Rust-dep CVE alerts — F3 resolves and closes the silent-vuln-on-dep-graph risk path.

entries.headline: Install via your OS's official channel (homebrew / winget / distro package)
entries.non_technical: wezterm is distributed through standard channels. macOS: brew install wezterm. Windows: winget install wezterm. Linux: distro package or AppImage from GitHub Releases.
entries.command: brew install wezterm  # macOS; use winget on Windows or AppImage from Releases on Linux

entries.headline: Understand what you're installing — wezterm handles SSH keys and can spawn processes via your Lua config
entries.non_technical: wezterm has an SSH client + Lua-scriptable config that can run child processes. This is the design. If you run third-party .wezterm.lua snippets, inspect them before sourcing.
entries.command: less ~/.wezterm.lua  # read your Lua config before trusting new snippets

entries.headline: Run `cargo audit` if building from source
entries.non_technical: Because dependabot tracks github-actions only, the Rust dep graph has no automated CVE watch. Cargo audit surfaces known advisories against Cargo.lock locally.
entries.command: git clone https://github.com/wezterm/wezterm && cd wezterm && cargo install cargo-audit && cargo audit

entries.headline: If you need recent main changes, build from source — the last release is 807 days old
entries.non_technical: The stable releases homebrew/winget/distros serve are from February 2024. Main is still active. If a fix you care about is on main but not in 20240203, build from source.

entries.headline: Keep SSH keys out of the wezterm-config scope if possible
entries.non_technical: wezterm-ssh handles your SSH keys for its built-in SSH client. If you prefer to keep SSH outside the terminal process, use a standalone SSH client and let wezterm just be the terminal.
entries.command: ssh user@host  # use system ssh instead of `wezterm ssh`

entries.label: Issue #5074 opened (Winget Defender FP)
entries.note: Still open 785 days later — FP per E8

entries.label: Last push to main
entries.note: Main active, 807 days post last release

executable_file_inventory.lead: wezterm ships multi-platform release binaries (Windows installer, macOS .dmg, Linux AppImage + distro packages) built from a 70+ crate Cargo workspace. Users install the GUI binary + get CLI helpers (`wezterm`, `wezterm-mux-server`, `wezterm-gui`, etc.) on PATH.
executable_file_inventory.layer1_summary: Primary surface is the compiled Rust binary family. Repo is a Cargo workspace; `wezterm-gui` is the GUI front; `wezterm-mux-server` is the multiplexer server; `wezterm-ssh` is the SSH client; 60+ more sub-crates provide escape parsing, Lua bindings, config, etc.

layer2_entries.dangerous_calls: SSH authentication incl. key-based; session multiplexing; remote command exec.
layer2_entries.notes: Handles the user's SSH keys for wezterm's built-in SSH client (`wezterm ssh user@host`). Privileged — a compromise here exposes the user's entire SSH key set.

layer2_entries.dangerous_calls: wezterm.run_child_process / background_child_process — Lua config can spawn arbitrary subprocesses.
layer2_entries.notes: Lua config surface. The user's .wezterm.lua can run arbitrary commands on the local machine. Not a bug — it's the documented extension mechanism — but the blast radius includes 'third-party wezterm.lua snippets the user copies from the internet'.

layer2_entries.dangerous_calls: Unix-socket IPC to multiplex terminal sessions across processes / over SSH.
layer2_entries.notes: Multiplexer server. Allows attaching/detaching sessions; enables wezterm to run detached from any specific GUI.

executable_file_inventory.closing_note: Installation is via official release binary or distro package — no curl-pipe installer. The primary supply-chain path is the GitHub Actions release pipeline (when it produces a new release, which hasn't happened in 807 days — see F1).

rows.what: feat: add config option for titlebar style (sample)
rows.author: external-contrib

rows.what: fix: handle wayland fractional scaling edge case (sample)
rows.author: external-contrib

pr_sample_review.intro: Representative rows from the 300-PR closed-and-merged sample. Full sample: 3.3% formal review, 32.7% any-review, 6 self-merges. Any-review rate is higher than typical solo-maintainer repos because wezterm has an active community commenting on PRs — but formal APPROVED decisions remain rare.

rows.note: GitHub couldn't classify; repo has LICENSE.md
Multi-platform CI: Linux/macOS/Windows continuous + tag builds
Low self-merge; wez merges external PRs

rows.value: GitHub Releases binaries + homebrew + winget + distro

rows.result: ⚠ Admin scope (403); dependabot.yml present — tracks github-actions only
⚠ 70+ crates.io channels surfaced from workspace sub-crates (F5) — real channels (GitHub Releases + homebrew + winget) NOT detected
⚠ 9 FP deserialization hits (Rust serde trait impls), 21 exec hits (mix of legitimate + FP), 1 FP weak_crypto (unicode name 'HANGUL SYLLABLE DES')
⊗ Not available on this scanner host

entries: Rust Cargo.lock parsing not implemented — the 70+ workspace sub-crates depend on an unscanned external dep graph.

Distribution-channel harness misinterpreted 70+ workspace sub-crates as publishable crates.io channels. The real distribution (GitHub Releases binaries + homebrew + winget + distro packages) was not detected — same pattern as Go (Xray-core) and Ruby (kamal).
Dependabot-alerts API required admin scope; dependabot.yml tracks github-actions only — Cargo ecosystem has no automated CVE watch.

Dangerous-primitives deserialization regex false-positives on Rust serde `fn deserialize` trait implementations (9 such hits). Same class of regex-calibration gap as on Go (Xray-core) and Python (Kronos pickle.load).
Gitleaks secret-scanning not available on this scanner host.
OSSF Scorecard not indexed — governance derived from raw gh api data.

Release-integrity grading is coarse — the harness doesn't check release assets for .sig / .asc / .dgst / checksum presence on wezterm releases; Phase 4 would need to fetch release data to attest artifact_verified.

does_anyone_check_the_code.short_answer: No — solo-maintainer (wez 97%), 3.3% formal review, no branch protection on a 25k-star privileged tool

do_they_fix_problems_quickly.short_answer: No — 807-day release stall (last tag 2024-02-03, main active through 2026-04-01). Fix-to-user latency is 26 months.

do_they_tell_you_about_problems.short_answer: Partly — CONTRIBUTING present, but no SECURITY.md and 0 published advisories in 8 years on a privileged tool

is_it_safe_out_of_the_box.short_answer: Partly — release artifacts on GitHub + homebrew + winget, no documented integrity verification; you're installing 2-year-old shipped code

catalog_metadata.short_description: GPU-accelerated cross-platform terminal emulator + multiplexer + SSH client written in Rust. 8-year-old project maintained near-solo by @wez. 25k stars, 64 releases, Cargo workspace with 70+ sub-crates.
catalog_metadata.target_user: Developer installing wezterm as their daily terminal emulator. Install path: GitHub Releases binary (Windows installer, macOS .dmg, Linux AppImage) + homebrew + winget + distro packages. Typical use: long-lived local terminal, optional SSH client, optional Lua-scripted config.
catalog_metadata.shape: Rust terminal emulator GUI + multiplexer + SSH client + Lua config (new shape — first Rust V1.2 entry in catalog; second terminal-emulator after ghostty entry 16; complements ghostty for V13-3 terminal-emulator cross-shape comparison).
catalog_metadata.prior_scan_note: None — first scan of wezterm. Sixth wild V1.2-schema scan (after markitdown 15, ghostty 16, Kronos 17, kamal 18, Xray-core 19, browser_terminal 20).

phase_4_structured_llm.timeline_intro: wezterm's public lifecycle — 8-year solo-maintained project, stable release cadence until early 2024 then a stall.
phase_4_structured_llm.evidence_intro: Command-backed evidence from Phase 1 harness + gh api queries + Cargo workspace inspection + issue #5074 review.
phase_4_structured_llm.coverage_intro: Phase 1 harness coverage with 7 documented gaps — most concentrated on Rust-ecosystem parsing (Cargo.lock, workspace sub-crate disambiguation, serde regex tuning).

editorial_caption.text: wezterm — 25k-star Rust terminal emulator + SSH client, near-solo by @wez (97.2% share). 30 CI workflows; zero branch protection; zero CODEOWNERS. Last release 2024-02-03 (807 days before scan) despite main active through 2026-04-01 — users on homebrew/winget/distro-stable run 2-year-old code. 0 advisories in 8 years. Zero scorecard overrides.

body_paragraphs: wez is the dominant author of wezterm by a wide margin — 7,346 commits of ~7,558 top-contributor total (97.2% share), with the next contributor (bew) at 65 commits. This is the most concentrated single-maintainer profile in the V1.2 catalog so far; it exceeds kamal's DHH+djmb duo (85% combined share) and the Xray-core maintainer (31% share with dependabot).

The tool's blast radius is substantial. wezterm-ssh handles user SSH keys for the built-in `wezterm ssh` client. Lua config (via `wezterm.run_child_process` / `background_child_process`) lets users spawn arbitrary subprocesses — a feature, but also a surface for malicious third-party config snippets. The multiplexer server uses Unix sockets. No branch protection at any layer, no CODEOWNERS, no ruleset. The 807-day release stall (F1) is what keeps this Warning rather than Critical — code doesn't reach users quickly, which blunts the governance exposure.

Consumer-side mitigation: pin to a specific release tag you reviewed; audit third-party .wezterm.lua snippets before sourcing them; prefer the system ssh client for sensitive targets rather than wezterm's built-in SSH. Maintainer-side: adding CODEOWNERS + a ruleset requiring ≥1 approving review would be a meaningful structural change — there are plausible co-maintainer candidates in the tail of contributors.

The release timeline from `gh api releases?per_page=10` is striking: `20240203-110809-5046fc22` published 2024-02-03, then `20240128-202157-1e552d76` (2024-01-29), `20240127-113634-bbcac864` (2024-01-27), `20230712-072601-f4abf8fd` (2023-07-12), and so on. Pre-February-2024 the cadence was roughly every 2 months. Then it stopped. Not slowed — stopped.

Main is NOT stopped. Last push 2026-04-01, 19 days before scan. 237 open PRs, 1,452 open issues, active community discussion. The release-tag step at the end of the CI pipeline is what has stalled, not development. The 30-workflow CI pipeline (F4) is still green on every push.

The consumer-facing effect: anyone who installed wezterm via winget, homebrew, or a distro stable package is running February 2024 code. Whatever fixes have landed on main over the last 807 days — and there have been many, given the push velocity — are not reaching installed users. If you need recent main fixes, build from source with `cargo install --git https://github.com/wezterm/wezterm wezterm-gui`. This is a material consumer-impact finding, not a governance-structure one.

community/profile reports has_security_policy=false and has_code_of_conduct=false (but has_contributing=true). The security-advisories endpoint returns 0 across 8 years of development on a 25k-star privileged tool that handles SSH keys and exposes Lua subprocess primitives. Three readings are plausible: (a) no security-relevant issues have been discovered — highly implausible; (b) fixes have shipped silently in releases — likely given the formerly-regular release cadence and ssh/lua surface area; (c) issues have been addressed without public disclosure.

Reading (b) matches the silent-fix pattern flagged on Kronos (entry 17) and Xray-core (entry 19). For wezterm specifically, the 807-day release stall (F1) complicates the picture — even if fixes ship to main, they don't reach users who installed before 2024-02-04. No documented private-disclosure channel means a future reporter with a pre-patched finding has no signposted path; GitHub's `Report a vulnerability` UI remains available as an implicit fallback.

Consumer implication: do not rely on the GitHub Security Advisory feed to learn about wezterm fixes — there is no feed. Monitor release notes directly when releases exist; subscribe to the repo for commit feeds if running from main.

dependabot.yml is present but its `ecosystems_tracked` list contains only `github-actions`. The Rust Cargo ecosystem is absent — despite the repo being a 70+ sub-crate workspace with transitive dep graphs that include serde, tokio, openssl, harfbuzz, cairo-sys-rs, fontconfig, freetype, and many more. A CVE published against any of these would not surface to the maintainer via dependabot.

This is the same operational-gap pattern seen on kamal (entry 18, Ruby Gemfile unwatched) and Xray-core (entry 19, Go-module alerts only partial). Consumer-side mitigation is simple: `cargo install cargo-audit && cargo audit` against the repo after cloning surfaces RustSec advisories against Cargo.lock. Maintainer-side: one YAML stanza adds `package-ecosystem: cargo` to dependabot.yml.

30 GitHub Actions workflows cover Linux/macOS/Windows continuous and tagged-release pipelines, nightly builds, pages deployment, and docs generation. This is above-average CI investment — substantially more automation than typical single-maintainer Rust repos. The signal here is that the engineering infrastructure is healthy. The gap in F1 (release stall) isn't 'CI is broken'; it's 'the release-tag step at the end of the pipeline stopped being invoked', which is a human-process decision rather than a tooling failure.
No pull_request_target usage across 30 workflows — that's a clean CI posture by modern Actions security standards. No evidence of stale action references (confirmed by dependabot on actions).

Three Rust-ecosystem scanner gaps surfaced on this scan: (1) Cargo.lock parsing not implemented — runtime_count=0 and osv total_vulns=0 are coverage artifacts; (2) distribution-channel detection misinterpreted 70+ workspace sub-crates as publishable crates.io channels, when the real channels are GitHub Releases binaries + homebrew + winget + distro packages (none of which the harness recognizes); (3) dangerous_primitives deserialization regex produced 9 false positives on Rust serde `fn deserialize` trait implementations — matching a substring that coincidentally exists in every serde-Deserialize impl.

Same class of gaps we saw on Go (Xray-core, entry 19) and Ruby (kamal, entry 18) — language-ecosystem-specific harness tuning. Harness-side fixes: (a) add Cargo.lock parsing with RustSec advisory-db lookup; (b) disambiguate workspace sub-crates from publishable channels by checking Cargo.toml `[workspace]` membership; (c) tighten the deserialization regex to avoid matching Rust serde trait-method signatures with generic parameters.
